AI.result: The results show that the proposed hierarchical and safety-aware approach achieves longer episode survival, lower peak line loading, and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen grids, outperforming flat reinforcement learning policies and safety-only methods.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that safety and generalization in power-grid control are best achieved through architectural design rather than increasingly complex reward engineering, providing a practical path toward deployable learning-based controllers for real-world energy systems.

title: Unleashing Implicit Rewards: Prefix-Value Learning for Distribution-Level Optimization
summary: Process reward models (PRMs) provide fine-grained reward signals along the reasoning process, but training reliable PRMs often requires step annotations or heavy verification pipelines, making them expensive to scale and refresh during online RL. Implicit PRMs mitigate this cost by learning decomposable token- or step-level rewards from trajectory-level outcome labels. However, they suffer from a train-inference mismatch: training only constrains a sequence-level aggregate, whereas inference requires token-level scores to reflect local step quality. As a result, token-level credits are weakly identified and may fail to faithfully reflect which reasoning steps are actually correct. This unreliability undermines a key promise of implicit PRMs: scoring many candidate tokens. In practice, noisy per-token advantages may systematically reinforce incorrect continuations. We address this problem with a novel Implicit Prefix-Value Reward Model (IPVRM), which directly learns a prefix-conditioned value function estimating the probability of eventual correctness, and derives step signals via temporal-difference (TD) differences. IPVRM substantially improves step-verification F1 on ProcessBench. Building on these calibrated prefix values, we further propose Distribution-Level RL (DistRL), which computes TD advantages for both sampled tokens and high-probability candidate tokens, enabling dense counterfactual updates without additional rollouts. While DistRL offers limited gains when powered by miscalibrated implicit rewards, it consistently improves downstream reasoning once paired with IPVRM.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel Implicit Prefix-Value Reward Model (IPVRM) and Distribution-Level RL (DistRL) to improve the reliability of implicit process reward models (PRMs) in reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Implicit PRMs suffer from train-inference mismatch, leading to unreliable token-level scores, which undermines their key promise of scoring many candidate tokens.
AI.method: The paper proposes IPVRM, which learns a prefix-conditioned value function and derives step signals via temporal-difference (TD) differences, and DistRL, which computes TD advantages for both sampled tokens and high-probability candidate tokens.
AI.result: IPVRM substantially improves step-verification F1 on ProcessBench, and DistRL consistently improves downstream reasoning when paired with IPVRM.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a solution to address the train-inference mismatch issue in implicit PRMs, leading to more reliable token-level scores and improved performance in reinforcement learning.

title: The Spectrascapes Dataset: Street-view imagery beyond the visible captured using a mobile platform
summary: High-resolution data in spatial and temporal contexts is imperative for developing climate resilient cities. Current datasets for monitoring urban parameters are developed primarily using manual inspections, embedded-sensing, remote sensing, or standard street-view imagery (RGB). These methods and datasets are often constrained respectively by poor scalability, inconsistent spatio-temporal resolutions, overhead views or low spectral information. We present a novel method and its open implementation: a multi-spectral terrestrial-view dataset that circumvents these limitations. This dataset consists of 17,718 street level multi-spectral images captured with RGB, Near-infrared, and Thermal imaging sensors on bikes, across diverse urban morphologies (village, town, small city, and big urban area) in the Netherlands. Strict emphasis is put on data calibration and quality while also providing the details of our data collection methodology (including the hardware and software details). To the best of our knowledge, Spectrascapes is the first open-access dataset of its kind. Finally, we demonstrate two downstream use-cases enabled using this dataset and provide potential research directions in the machine learning, urban planning and remote sensing domains.

AI.tldr: A novel multi-spectral terrestrial-view dataset, Spectrascapes, is presented, which provides high-resolution urban data to support climate-resilient city development.
AI.motivation: Current urban data collection methods have limitations such as poor scalability, inconsistent resolutions, and limited spectral information, highlighting the need for a more comprehensive dataset.
AI.method: A multi-spectral terrestrial-view dataset was created using bikes equipped with RGB, Near-infrared, and Thermal imaging sensors to capture 17,718 street-level images across various urban morphologies in the Netherlands.
AI.result: The Spectrascapes dataset, an open-access dataset, is presented along with its data collection methodology and two potential downstream use-cases in machine learning, urban planning, and remote sensing.
AI.conclusion: The Spectrascapes dataset has the potential to support climate-resilient city development by providing high-resolution urban data, and future research directions are proposed in machine learning, urban planning, and remote sensing domains.

title: TREX: Automating LLM Fine-tuning via Agent-Driven Tree-based Exploration
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have empowered AI research agents to perform isolated scientific tasks, automating complex, real-world workflows, such as LLM training, remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we introduce TREX, a multi-agent system that automates the entire LLM training life-cycle. By orchestrating collaboration between two core modules-the Researcher and the Executor-the system seamlessly performs requirement analysis, open-domain literature and data research, formulation of training strategies, preparation of data recipes, and model training and evaluation. The multi-round experimental process is modeled as a search tree, enabling the system to efficiently plan exploration paths, reuse historical results, and distill high-level insights from iterative trials. To evaluate the capability of automated LLM training, we construct FT-Bench, a benchmark comprising 10 tasks derived from real-world scenarios, ranging from optimizing fundamental model capabilities to enhancing performance on domain-specific tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the TREX agent consistently optimizes model performance on target tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TREX, a multi-agent system that automates the entire Large Language Model (LLM) training life-cycle, enabling efficient and optimized model performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of automating complex, real-world workflows such as LLM training, which currently requires manual intervention.
AI.method: The method involves a multi-agent system called TREX, which consists of two core modules: Researcher and Executor. These modules collaborate to perform requirement analysis, literature and data research, formulation of training strategies, data preparation, and model training and evaluation.
AI.result: The experimental results show that the TREX agent consistently optimizes model performance on target tasks, as evaluated on the FT-Bench benchmark comprising 10 real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that TREX effectively automates the LLM training life-cycle, leading to improved model performance and efficiency in complex, real-world workflows.

title: InfiniteScienceGym: An Unbounded, Procedurally-Generated Benchmark for Scientific Analysis
summary: Large language models are emerging as scientific assistants, but evaluating their ability to reason from empirical data remains challenging. Benchmarks derived from published studies and human annotations inherit publication bias, known-knowledge bias, label noise, and substantial storage requirements. We present InfiniteScienceGym, a procedurally generated benchmark of scientific repositories paired with a verifiable question-answering task. From a seed, the simulator deterministically generates a self-contained repository with realistic directory structure, files, and tabular data, and a privileged QA generator produces both answerable and unanswerable questions with exact ground truth. This makes it possible to evaluate evidence-grounded reasoning, abstention, and tool-mediated analysis in a controlled setting without distributing a large static corpus. InfiniteScienceGym complements real scientific benchmarks by targeting blind spots and failure modes that are hard to evaluate using published datasets alone. Evaluating both proprietary and open-weight models, we find that none achieve more than 45% accuracy overall, that recognizing unanswerable questions remains a major weakness, and that stronger models tend to use tools more effectively rather than simply consuming more tokens.

AI.tldr: The authors present InfiniteScienceGym, a new benchmark for evaluating large language models' ability to reason from empirical data. They find that even proprietary and open-weight models struggle, achieving only up to 45% accuracy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges of evaluating large language models' ability to reason from empirical data, which is crucial for their role as scientific assistants. They identify limitations in existing benchmarks, such as publication bias and label noise.
AI.method: The authors propose InfiniteScienceGym, a procedurally generated benchmark that simulates scientific repositories with realistic directory structures, files, and tabular data. They use a privileged QA generator to produce both answerable and unanswerable questions with exact ground truth.
AI.result: The authors evaluate proprietary and open-weight models on InfiniteScienceGym and find that none achieve more than 45% accuracy overall. They also identify that recognizing unanswerable questions remains a major weakness and that stronger models tend to use tools more effectively.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that InfiniteScienceGym complements real scientific benchmarks by targeting blind spots and failure modes that are hard to evaluate using published datasets alone. They highlight the need for further improvement in large language models' ability to reason from empirical data.

title: Why MLLMs Struggle to Determine Object Orientations
summary:
Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with tasks that require reasoning about 2D object orientation in images, as documented in prior work. Tong et al. and Nichols et al. hypothesize that these failures originate in the visual encoder, since commonly used encoders such as CLIP and SigLIP are trained for image-text semantic alignment rather than geometric reasoning. We design a controlled empirical protocol to test this claim by measuring whether rotations can be recovered from encoder representations. In particular, we examine SigLIP and ViT features from LLaVA OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct models, respectively, using full images, and examine CLIP representations in LLaVA 1.5 and 1.6 using rotated foreground patches against natural background images. Our null hypothesis is that orientation information is not preserved in the encoder embeddings and we test this by training linear regressors to predict object orientation from encoded features. Contrary to the hypothesis, we find that orientation information is recoverable from encoder representations: simple linear models accurately predict object orientations from embeddings. This contradicts the assumption that MLLM orientation failures originate in the visual encoder.
  Having rejected the accepted hypothesis that MLLMs struggle with 2D orientation tasks because of visual encoder limitations, we still don't know why they fail. Although a full explanation is beyond the scope of this paper, we show that although present, orientation information is spread diffusely across tens of thousands of features. This may or may not be while MLLMs fail to exploit the available orientation information.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with tasks requiring 2D object orientation reasoning in images. It challenges the hypothesis that visual encoder limitations are the cause and finds that orientation information is recoverable from encoder representations, but MLLMs may fail to exploit this information.
AI.motivation: MLLMs have been documented to struggle with tasks requiring 2D object orientation reasoning in images. Previous work hypothesized that these failures originate in the visual encoder due to its training for image-text semantic alignment rather than geometric reasoning.
AI.method: The authors design a controlled empirical protocol to test the hypothesis. They measure whether rotations can be recovered from encoder representations by training linear regressors to predict object orientation from encoded features of various models, including LLaVA OneVision, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, LLaVA 1.5, and 1.6.
AI.result: Contrary to the hypothesis, the authors find that orientation information is recoverable from encoder representations. Simple linear models accurately predict object orientations from embeddings, suggesting that MLLM orientation failures do not originate in the visual encoder. However, the orientation information is spread diffusely across tens of thousands of features.
AI.conclusion: The study contradicts the assumption that MLLM orientation failures originate in the visual encoder. While orientation information is present in encoder representations, MLLMs may still fail to exploit this information, but the reason for their failure remains unexplained in this paper.

title: Evaluating the Evaluator: Problems with SemEval-2020 Task 1 for Lexical Semantic Change Detection
summary: This discussion paper re-examines SemEval-2020 Task 1, the most influential shared benchmark for lexical semantic change detection, through a three-part evaluative framework: operationalisation, data quality, and benchmark design. First, at the level of operationalisation, we argue that the benchmark models semantic change mainly as gain, loss, or redistribution of discrete senses. While practical for annotation and evaluation, this framing is too narrow to capture gradual, constructional, collocational, and discourse-level change. Also, the gold labels are outcomes of annotation decisions, clustering procedures, and threshold settings, which could potentially limit the validity of the task. Second, at the level of data quality, we show that the benchmark is affected by substantial corpus and preprocessing problems, including OCR noise, malformed characters, truncated sentences, inconsistent lemmatisation, POS-tagging errors, and missed targets. These issues can distort model behaviour, complicate linguistic analysis, and reduce reproducibility. Third, at the level of bench-mark design, we argue the small curated target sets and limited language coverage reduce realism and increase statistical uncertainty. Taken together, these limitations suggest that the benchmark should be treated as a useful but partial test bed rather than a definitive measure of progress. We therefore call for future datasets and shared tasks to adopt broader theories of semantic change, document pre-processing transparently, expand cross-linguistic coverage, and use more realistic evaluation settings. Such steps are necessary for more valid, interpretable, and generalisable progress in lexical semantic change detection

AI.tldr: The paper re-examines the SemEval-2020 Task 1 benchmark for lexical semantic change detection, highlighting limitations in operationalisation, data quality, and benchmark design.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to critically evaluate the SemEval-2020 Task 1 benchmark to identify areas for improvement in lexical semantic change detection.
AI.method: The authors use a three-part evaluative framework: operationalisation, data quality, and benchmark design, to assess the limitations of the benchmark.
AI.result: The authors identify several limitations, including narrow operationalisation of semantic change, data quality issues, and limitations in benchmark design, which affect the validity, interpretability, and generalisability of progress in lexical semantic change detection.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the benchmark should be treated as a useful but partial test bed rather than a definitive measure of progress, and call for future datasets and shared tasks to adopt broader theories of semantic change, document pre-processing transparently, expand cross-linguistic coverage, and use more realistic evaluation settings.

title: Towards Successful Implementation of Automated Raveling Detection: Effects of Training Data Size, Illumination Difference, and Spatial Shift
summary: Raveling, the loss of aggregates, is a major form of asphalt pavement surface distress, especially on highways. While research has shown that machine learning and deep learning-based methods yield promising results for raveling detection by classification on range images, their performance often degrades in large-scale deployments where more diverse inference data may originate from different runs, sensors, and environmental conditions. This degradation highlights the need of a more generalizable and robust solution for real-world implementation. Thus, the objectives of this study are to 1) identify and assess potential variations that impact model robustness, such as the quantity of training data, illumination difference, and spatial shift; and 2) leverage findings to enhance model robustness under real-world conditions. To this end, we propose RavelingArena, a benchmark designed to evaluate model robustness to variations in raveling detection. Instead of collecting extensive new data, it is built by augmenting an existing dataset with diverse, controlled variations, thereby enabling variation-controlled experiments to quantify the impact of each variation. Results demonstrate that both the quantity and diversity of training data are critical to the accuracy of models, achieving at least a 9.2% gain in accuracy under the most diverse conditions in experiments. Additionally, a case study applying these findings to a multi-year test section in Georgia, U.S., shows significant improvements in year-to-year consistency, laying foundations for future studies on temporal deterioration modeling. These insights provide guidance for more reliable model deployment in raveling detection and other real-world tasks that require adaptability to diverse conditions.

AI.tldr: The study aims to improve the robustness of machine learning models for detecting raveling, a major form of asphalt pavement surface distress, under diverse real-world conditions. A benchmark called RavelingArena is proposed to evaluate model robustness to variations.
AI.motivation: The performance of machine learning models for raveling detection degrades in large-scale deployments due to diverse inference data from different runs, sensors, and environmental conditions. This highlights the need for a more generalizable and robust solution.
AI.method: The study proposes RavelingArena, a benchmark built by augmenting an existing dataset with diverse, controlled variations. This allows for variation-controlled experiments to quantify the impact of each variation on model robustness.
AI.result: The results show that both the quantity and diversity of training data are critical to model accuracy, achieving at least a 9.2% gain in accuracy under the most diverse conditions. A case study in Georgia, U.S. demonstrates significant improvements in year-to-year consistency.
AI.conclusion: The study provides guidance for more reliable model deployment in raveling detection and other real-world tasks that require adaptability to diverse conditions. The proposed benchmark and findings can help improve the robustness of machine learning models under real-world conditions.

title: Hessian-Enhanced Token Attribution (HETA): Interpreting Autoregressive LLMs
summary: Attribution methods seek to explain language model predictions by quantifying the contribution of input tokens to generated outputs. However, most existing techniques are designed for encoder-based architectures and rely on linear approximations that fail to capture the causal and semantic complexities of autoregressive generation in decoder-only models. To address these limitations, we propose Hessian-Enhanced Token Attribution (HETA), a novel attribution framework tailored for decoder-only language models. HETA combines three complementary components: a semantic transition vector that captures token-to-token influence across layers, Hessian-based sensitivity scores that model second-order effects, and KL divergence to measure information loss when tokens are masked. This unified design produces context-aware, causally faithful, and semantically grounded attributions. Additionally, we introduce a curated benchmark dataset for systematically evaluating attribution quality in generative settings. Empirical evaluations across multiple models and datasets demonstrate that HETA consistently outperforms existing methods in attribution faithfulness and alignment with human annotations, establishing a new standard for interpretability in autoregressive language models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hessian-Enhanced Token Attribution (HETA), a novel framework for explaining language model predictions in decoder-only models, outperforming existing methods in faithfulness and alignment with human annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing attribution methods are limited in their ability to capture causal and semantic complexities of autoregressive generation in decoder-only models, and the authors aim to address these limitations.
AI.method: The authors propose HETA, which combines a semantic transition vector, Hessian-based sensitivity scores, and KL divergence to produce context-aware, causally faithful, and semantically grounded attributions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate that HETA consistently outperforms existing methods in attribution faithfulness and alignment with human annotations across multiple models and datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HETA framework establishes a new standard for interpretability in autoregressive language models, providing a more accurate and reliable method for explaining language model predictions.

title: Right Regions, Wrong Labels: Semantic Label Flips in Segmentation under Correlation Shift
comment: Accepted at the CAO Workshop, ICLR 2026
summary: The robustness of machine learning models can be compromised by spurious correlations between non-causal features in the input data and target labels. A common way to test for such correlations is to train on data where the label is strongly tied to some non-causal cue, then evaluate on examples where that tie no longer holds. This idea is well established for classification tasks, but for semantic segmentation the specific failure modes are not well understood. We show that a model may achieve reasonable overlap while assigning the wrong semantic label, swapping one plausible foreground class for another, even when object boundaries are largely correct. We focus on this semantic label-flip behaviour and quantify it with a simple diagnostic (Flip) that counts how often ground truth foreground pixels are assigned the wrong foreground identity while remaining predicted as foreground. In a setting where category and scene are correlated during training, increasing the correlation consistently widens the gap between common and rare test conditions and increases these within-object label swaps on counterfactual groups. Overall, our results motivate assessing segmentation robustness under distribution shift beyond overlap by decomposing foreground errors into correct pixels, flipped-identity pixels, and missed-to-background pixels. We also propose an entropy-based, ground truth label-free `flip-risk' score, which is computed from foreground identity uncertainty, and show that it can flag flip-prone cases at inference time. Code is available at https://github.com/acharaakshit/label-flips.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the robustness of machine learning models in semantic segmentation tasks, particularly when dealing with spurious correlations between non-causal features and target labels. It introduces a diagnostic tool to quantify semantic label-flip behavior and proposes an entropy-based score to flag flip-prone cases at inference time.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to investigate the robustness of machine learning models in semantic segmentation tasks when faced with spurious correlations between non-causal features and target labels, which can lead to incorrect semantic labels being assigned.
AI.method: The authors propose a diagnostic tool called 'Flip' to quantify semantic label-flip behavior, which counts the number of times ground truth foreground pixels are assigned the wrong foreground identity while still being predicted as foreground. They also introduce an entropy-based, ground truth label-free 'flip-risk' score to flag flip-prone cases at inference time.
AI.result: The results show that increasing the correlation between category and scene during training widens the gap between common and rare test conditions and increases within-object label swaps on counterfactual groups. The proposed 'flip-risk' score is able to flag flip-prone cases at inference time.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that assessing segmentation robustness under distribution shift is crucial beyond overlap by decomposing foreground errors into correct pixels, flipped-identity pixels, and missed-to-background pixels. The proposed diagnostic tool and 'flip-risk' score can help in identifying and mitigating semantic label-flip behavior in semantic segmentation models.

title: Better and Worse with Scale: How Contextual Entrainment Diverges with Model Size
comment: 16 pages, 11 figures, 6 tables. Accepted to Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Larger language models become simultaneously better and worse at handling contextual information -- better at ignoring false claims, worse at ignoring irrelevant tokens. We formalize this apparent paradox through the first scaling laws for contextual entrainment, the tendency of models to favor tokens that appeared in context regardless of relevance. Analyzing the Cerebras-GPT (111M-13B) and Pythia (410M-12B) model families, we find entrainment follows predictable power-law scaling, but with opposite trends depending on context type: semantic contexts show decreasing entrainment with scale, while non-semantic contexts show increasing entrainment. Concretely, the largest models are four times more resistant to counterfactual misinformation than the smallest, yet simultaneously twice as prone to copying arbitrary tokens. These diverging trends, which replicate across model families, suggest that semantic filtering and mechanical copying are functionally distinct behaviors that scale in opposition -- scaling alone does not resolve context sensitivity, it reshapes it.

AI.tldr: Larger language models exhibit a paradoxical behavior, becoming better at ignoring false claims but worse at ignoring irrelevant tokens, with scaling laws revealing distinct trends for semantic and non-semantic contexts.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to formalize and understand the apparent paradox of larger language models' performance on contextual information, specifically their tendency to favor tokens that appeared in context regardless of relevance.
AI.method: The authors analyze the Cerebras-GPT and Pythia model families, using scaling laws to study the trend of contextual entrainment across different model sizes and context types.
AI.result: The study finds that entrainment follows predictable power-law scaling, with semantic contexts showing decreasing entrainment and non-semantic contexts showing increasing entrainment with scale, leading to diverging trends in the largest models' behavior.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest that semantic filtering and mechanical copying are functionally distinct behaviors that scale in opposition, implying that scaling alone does not resolve context sensitivity, but rather reshapes it.

title: SSD-GS: Scattering and Shadow Decomposition for Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. Code available at: https://github.com/irisfreesiri/SSD-GS
summary: We present SSD-GS, a physically-based relighting framework built upon 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that achieves high-quality reconstruction and photorealistic relighting under novel lighting conditions. In physically-based relighting, accurately modeling light-material interactions is essential for faithful appearance reproduction. However, existing 3DGS-based relighting methods adopt coarse shading decompositions, either modeling only diffuse and specular reflections or relying on neural networks to approximate shadows and scattering. This leads to limited fidelity and poor physical interpretability, particularly for anisotropic metals and translucent materials. To address these limitations, SSD-GS decomposes reflectance into four components: diffuse, specular, shadow, and subsurface scattering. We introduce a learnable dipole-based scattering module for subsurface transport, an occlusion-aware shadow formulation that integrates visibility estimates with a refinement network, and an enhanced specular component with an anisotropic Fresnel-based model. Through progressive integration of all components during training, SSD-GS effectively disentangles lighting and material properties, even for unseen illumination conditions, as demonstrated on the challenging OLAT dataset. Experiments demonstrate superior quantitative and perceptual relighting quality compared to prior methods and pave the way for downstream tasks, including controllable light source editing and interactive scene relighting. The source code is available at: https://github.com/irisfreesiri/SSD-GS.

AI.tldr: A novel relighting framework called SSD-GS is proposed, which achieves high-quality reconstruction and photorealistic relighting under novel lighting conditions by accurately modeling light-material interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS-based relighting methods have limited fidelity and poor physical interpretability due to coarse shading decompositions, which leads to inaccurate modeling of light-material interactions.
AI.method: SSD-GS decomposes reflectance into four components: diffuse, specular, shadow, and subsurface scattering, and introduces a learnable dipole-based scattering module, an occlusion-aware shadow formulation, and an enhanced specular component.
AI.result: SSD-GS demonstrates superior quantitative and perceptual relighting quality compared to prior methods on the OLAT dataset and enables downstream tasks such as controllable light source editing and interactive scene relighting.
AI.conclusion: SSD-GS presents a physically-based relighting framework that achieves high-quality reconstruction and photorealistic relighting under novel lighting conditions, paving the way for various applications.

title: L2D-Clinical: Learning to Defer for Adaptive Model Selection in Clinical Text Classification
summary: Clinical text classification requires choosing between specialized fine-tuned models (BERT variants) and general-purpose large language models (LLMs), yet neither dominates across all instances. We introduce Learning to Defer for clinical text (L2D-Clinical), a framework that learns when a BERT classifier should defer to an LLM based on uncertainty signals and text characteristics. Unlike prior L2D work that defers to human experts assumed universally superior, our approach enables adaptive deferral-improving accuracy when the LLM complements BERT. We evaluate on two English clinical tasks: (1) ADE detection (ADE Corpus V2), where BioBERT (F1=0.911) outperforms the LLM (F1=0.765), and (2) treatment outcome classification (MIMIC-IV with multi-LLM consensus ground truth), where GPT-5-nano (F1=0.967) outperforms ClinicalBERT (F1=0.887). On ADE, L2D-Clinical achieves F1=0.928 (+1.7 points over BERT) by selectively deferring 7% of instances where the LLM's high recall compensates for BERT's misses. On MIMIC, L2D-Clinical achieves F1=0.980 (+9.3 points over BERT) by deferring only 16.8\% of cases to the LLM. The key insight is that L2D-Clinical learns to selectively leverage LLM strengths while minimizing API costs.

AI.tldr: Introduced L2D-Clinical, a framework that combines BERT and LLM for clinical text classification, achieving improved accuracy by selectively deferring to LLM.
AI.motivation: Clinical text classification requires choosing between specialized models (BERT variants) and general-purpose large language models (LLMs), but neither dominates across all instances.
AI.method: L2D-Clinical framework learns when a BERT classifier should defer to an LLM based on uncertainty signals and text characteristics.
AI.result: L2D-Clinical achieved F1=0.928 (+1.7 points over BERT) on ADE detection and F1=0.980 (+9.3 points over BERT) on treatment outcome classification.
AI.conclusion: L2D-Clinical learns to selectively leverage LLM strengths while minimizing API costs, improving accuracy in clinical text classification tasks.

title: SEDTalker: Emotion-Aware 3D Facial Animation Using Frame-Level Speech Emotion Diarization
summary: We introduce SEDTalker, an emotion-aware framework for speech-driven 3D facial animation that leverages frame-level speech emotion diarization to achieve fine-grained expressive control. Unlike prior approaches that rely on utterance-level or manually specified emotion labels, our method predicts temporally dense emotion categories and intensities directly from speech, enabling continuous modulation of facial expressions over time. The diarized emotion signals are encoded as learned embeddings and used to condition a speech-driven 3D animation model based on a hybrid Transformer-Mamba architecture. This design allows effective disentanglement of linguistic content and emotional style while preserving identity and temporal coherence. We evaluate our approach on a large-scale multi-corpus dataset for speech emotion diarization and on the EmoVOCA dataset for emotional 3D facial animation. Quantitative results demonstrate strong frame-level emotion recognition performance and low geometric and temporal reconstruction errors, while qualitative results show smooth emotion transitions and consistent expression control. These findings highlight the effectiveness of frame-level emotion diarization for expressive and controllable 3D talking head generation.

AI.tldr: SEDtalker is a framework for speech-driven 3D facial animation that uses frame-level speech emotion to achieve fine-grained expressive control.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enable continuous modulation of facial expressions over time by predicting temporally dense emotion categories and intensities directly from speech.
AI.method: The method uses frame-level speech emotion diarization, encodes the diarized emotion signals as learned embeddings, and conditions a speech-driven 3D animation model based on a hybrid Transformer-Mamba architecture.
AI.result: The results show strong frame-level emotion recognition performance, low geometric and temporal reconstruction errors, smooth emotion transitions, and consistent expression control.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively enables expressive and controllable 3D talking head generation using frame-level emotion diarization.

title: English is Not All You Need: Systematically Exploring the Role of Multilinguality in LLM Post-Training
summary: Despite the widespread multilingual deployment of large language models, post-training pipelines remain predominantly English-centric, contributing to performance disparities across languages. We present a systematic, controlled study of the interplay between training language coverage, model scale, and task domain, based on 220 supervised fine-tuning runs on parallel translated multilingual data mixtures spanning mathematical reasoning and API calling tasks, with models up to 8B parameters. We find that increasing language coverage during post-training is largely beneficial across tasks and model scales, with low-resource languages benefiting the most and high-resource languages plateauing rather than degrading. Even minimal multilinguality helps: incorporating a single non-English language improves both English performance and cross-lingual generalization, making English-only post-training largely suboptimal. Moreover, at sufficient language diversity, zero-shot cross-lingual transfer can match or exceed the effects of direct language inclusion in a low-diversity setting, although gains remain limited for typologically distant, low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The study examines how multilingual training affects large language models, finding that broader language coverage during post-training improves performance across tasks and model sizes, especially for low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this study is to address the performance disparities across languages in large language models due to predominantly English-centric post-training pipelines.
AI.method: The study conducts 220 supervised fine-tuning runs on parallel translated multilingual data mixtures for mathematical reasoning and API calling tasks, using models up to 8B parameters.
AI.result: The results show that increasing language coverage during post-training benefits performance across tasks and model scales, with low-resource languages benefiting the most. Even minimal multilinguality helps, and at sufficient language diversity, zero-shot cross-lingual transfer can match or exceed direct language inclusion.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that English-only post-training is largely suboptimal, and incorporating multiple languages can improve both English performance and cross-lingual generalization, particularly benefiting low-resource languages.

title: MSGS: Multispectral 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: We present a multispectral extension to 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for wavelength-aware view synthesis. Each Gaussian is augmented with spectral radiance, represented via per-band spherical harmonics, and optimized under a dual-loss supervision scheme combining RGB and multispectral signals. To improve rendering fidelity, we perform spectral-to-RGB conversion at the pixel level, allowing richer spectral cues to be retained during optimization. Our method is evaluated on both public and self-captured real-world datasets, demonstrating consistent improvements over the RGB-only 3DGS baseline in terms of image quality and spectral consistency. Notably, it excels in challenging scenes involving translucent materials and anisotropic reflections. The proposed approach maintains the compactness and real-time efficiency of 3DGS while laying the foundation for future integration with physically based shading models.

AI.tldr: A multispectral extension to 3D Gaussian Splatting for improved view synthesis, enhancing image quality and spectral consistency.
AI.motivation: To improve wavelength-aware view synthesis by incorporating multispectral information into 3D Gaussian Splatting.
AI.method: Augmenting Gaussians with spectral radiance, using per-band spherical harmonics, and optimizing under a dual-loss supervision scheme.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over RGB-only 3DGS baseline in image quality and spectral consistency, especially in scenes with translucent materials and anisotropic reflections.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enhances 3D Gaussian Splatting with multispectral capabilities, maintaining compactness and real-time efficiency while supporting future integration with physically based shading models.

title: Giving Voice to the Constitution: Low-Resource Text-to-Speech for Quechua and Spanish Using a Bilingual Legal Corpus
summary: We present a unified pipeline for synthesizing high-quality Quechua and Spanish speech for the Peruvian Constitution using three state-of-the-art text-to-speech (TTS) architectures: XTTS v2, F5-TTS, and DiFlow-TTS. Our models are trained on independent Spanish and Quechua speech datasets with heterogeneous sizes and recording conditions, and leverage bilingual and multilingual TTS capabilities to improve synthesis quality in both languages. By exploiting cross-lingual transfer, our framework mitigates data scarcity in Quechua while preserving naturalness in Spanish. We release trained checkpoints, inference code, and synthesized audio for each constitutional article, providing a reusable resource for speech technologies in indigenous and multilingual contexts. This work contributes to the development of inclusive TTS systems for political and legal content in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: A unified pipeline for synthesizing high-quality Quechua and Spanish speech for the Peruvian Constitution is presented, leveraging state-of-the-art TTS architectures and cross-lingual transfer to improve synthesis quality.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop inclusive TTS systems for political and legal content in low-resource settings, specifically for indigenous languages like Quechua.
AI.method: The method involves training TTS models on independent Spanish and Quechua speech datasets using three state-of-the-art architectures: XTTS v2, F5-TTS, and DiFlow-TTS, and exploiting cross-lingual transfer to mitigate data scarcity in Quechua.
AI.result: The result is a reusable resource for speech technologies in indigenous and multilingual contexts, including trained checkpoints, inference code, and synthesized audio for each constitutional article.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed framework contributes to the development of inclusive TTS systems for political and legal content in low-resource settings, preserving naturalness in Spanish while improving synthesis quality in Quechua.

title: Multi-Agent Object Detection Framework Based on Raspberry Pi YOLO Detector and Slack-Ollama Natural Language Interface
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables, implementation code will be made available upon manuscript publication
summary: The paper presents design and prototype implementation of an edge based object detection system within the new paradigm of AI agents orchestration. It goes beyond traditional design approaches by leveraging on LLM based natural language interface for system control and communication and practically demonstrates integration of all system components into a single resource constrained hardware platform. The method is based on the proposed multi-agent object detection framework which tightly integrates different AI agents within the same task of providing object detection and tracking capabilities. The proposed design principles highlight the fast prototyping approach that is characteristic for transformational potential of generative AI systems, which are applied during both development and implementation stages. Instead of specialized communication and control interface, the system is made by using Slack channel chatbot agent and accompanying Ollama LLM reporting agent, which are both run locally on the same Raspberry Pi platform, alongside the dedicated YOLO based computer vision agent performing real time object detection and tracking. Agent orchestration is implemented through a specially designed event based message exchange subsystem, which represents an alternative to completely autonomous agent orchestration and control characteristic for contemporary LLM based frameworks like the recently proposed OpenClaw. Conducted experimental investigation provides valuable insights into limitations of the low cost testbed platforms in the design of completely centralized multi-agent AI systems. The paper also discusses comparative differences between presented approach and the solution that would require additional cloud based external resources.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a design and prototype implementation of an edge-based object detection system using AI agents orchestration with a natural language interface.
AI.motivation: To leverage the potential of generative AI systems and overcome traditional design approaches by integrating different AI agents within the same task of object detection and tracking.
AI.method: A multi-agent object detection framework with a natural language interface using LLM, Slack channel chatbot agent, and Ollama LLM reporting agent, all running on a Raspberry Pi platform.
AI.result: The proposed system demonstrates the integration of all components into a single resource-constrained hardware platform, providing valuable insights into limitations of low-cost testbed platforms.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights the potential of the proposed design principles for fast prototyping and transformational potential of generative AI systems, and discusses comparative differences with cloud-based external resources.

title: AgentSPEX: An Agent SPecification and EXecution Language
summary: Language-model agent systems commonly rely on reactive prompting, in which a single instruction guides the model through an open-ended sequence of reasoning and tool-use steps, leaving control flow and intermediate state implicit and making agent behavior potentially difficult to control. Orchestration frameworks such as LangGraph, DSPy, and CrewAI impose greater structure through explicit workflow definitions, but tightly couple workflow logic with Python, making agents difficult to maintain and modify. In this paper, we introduce AgentSPEX, an Agent SPecification and EXecution Language for specifying LLM-agent workflows with explicit control flow and modular structure, along with a customizable agent harness. AgentSPEX supports typed steps, branching and loops, parallel execution, reusable submodules, and explicit state management, and these workflows execute within an agent harness that provides tool access, a sandboxed virtual environment, and support for checkpointing, verification, and logging. Furthermore, we provide a visual editor with synchronized graph and workflow views for authoring and inspection. We include ready-to-use agents for deep research and scientific research, and we evaluate AgentSPEX on 7 benchmarks. Finally, we show through a user study that AgentSPEX provides a more interpretable and accessible workflow-authoring paradigm than a popular existing agent framework.

AI.tldr: AgentSPEX is introduced as a language for specifying and executing LLM-agent workflows with explicit control flow and modular structure, providing features like typed steps, branching, parallel execution, and a visual editor.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the limitations of reactive prompting and existing orchestration frameworks, which can lead to difficulties in controlling agent behavior and maintaining/modifying agents.
AI.method: The authors introduce AgentSPEX, an Agent SPecification and EXecution Language, along with a customizable agent harness that provides features like tool access, sandboxed virtual environment, and support for checkpointing, verification, and logging.
AI.result: The evaluation of AgentSPEX on 7 benchmarks and a user study show that it provides a more interpretable and accessible workflow-authoring paradigm compared to a popular existing agent framework.
AI.conclusion: AgentSPEX offers a structured approach to specifying and executing LLM-agent workflows, enhancing control, maintainability, and interpretability of agent behavior.

title: A 3D SAM-Based Progressive Prompting Framework for Multi-Task Segmentation of Radiotherapy-induced Normal Tissue Injuries in Limited-Data Settings
summary: Radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injury is a clinically important complication, and accurate segmentation of injury regions from medical images could facilitate disease assessment, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring. However, automatic segmentation of these lesions remains largely unexplored because of limited voxel-level annotations and substantial heterogeneity across injury types, lesion size, and imaging modality. To address this gap, we curate a dedicated head-and-neck radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injury dataset covering three manifestations: osteoradionecrosis (ORN), cerebral edema (CE), and cerebral radiation necrosis (CRN). We further propose a 3D SAM-based progressive prompting framework for multi-task segmentation in limited-data settings. The framework progressively incorporates three complementary prompts: text prompts for task-aware adaptation, dose-guided box prompts for coarse localization, and click prompts for iterative refinement. A small-target focus loss is introduced to improve local prediction and boundary delineation for small and sparse lesions. Experiments on ORN, CE, and CRN demonstrate that the proposed method achieves reliable segmentation performance across diverse injury types and outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A novel framework for segmenting radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injuries in medical images is proposed, addressing the challenge of limited voxel-level annotations and heterogeneity across injury types.
AI.motivation: Radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injury is a significant complication, and accurate segmentation of injury regions is crucial for disease assessment, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring.
AI.method: A 3D SAM-based progressive prompting framework for multi-task segmentation in limited-data settings, incorporating text prompts, dose-guided box prompts, and click prompts, along with a small-target focus loss.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves reliable segmentation performance across diverse injury types (ORN, CE, and CRN) and outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework has the potential to facilitate accurate segmentation of radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injuries, which could improve disease assessment, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring.

title: Peer-Predictive Self-Training for Language Model Reasoning
summary: Mechanisms for continued self-improvement of language models without external supervision remain an open challenge. We propose Peer-Predictive Self-Training (PST), a label-free fine-tuning framework in which multiple language models improve collaboratively by leveraging a cross-model aggregated response as an internal training signal. Given a prompt question, the models generate responses sequentially; the final aggregated answer, often more reliable than individual responses in practice, serves as an internal target for learning. We measure how informative each intermediate response is about the aggregate using pointwise mutual information (PMI), and use this signal to scale self-training updates. Responses already aligned with the aggregate are updated less, while less informative or misaligned responses are updated more. On mathematical reasoning benchmarks (SimulEq, Math500, and MultiArith), PST improves exact-match accuracy by 2.2 to 4.3 percentage points across Gemma-2-2B, LLaMA-3.2-1B, and Qwen-2.5-1.5B, and reduces the average generator-verifier gap (GV-Gap) by 26 to 40 percent, while requiring no external supervision or teacher-student hierarchy and relying solely on cross-model interactions. These results suggest that cross-model generations and peer-predictive feedback can serve as an effective approach for self-supervised training.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel framework called Peer-Predictive Self-Training (PST) for self-improvement of language models without external supervision.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of continued self-improvement of language models without external supervision.
AI.method: The authors propose a label-free fine-tuning framework called Peer-Predictive Self-Training (PST), where multiple language models improve collaboratively by leveraging a cross-model aggregated response as an internal training signal.
AI.result: PST improves exact-match accuracy by 2.2 to 4.3 percentage points across Gemma-2-2B, LLaMA-3.2-1B, and Qwen-2.5-1.5B, and reduces the average generator-verifier gap (GV-Gap) by 26 to 40 percent.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest that cross-model generations and peer-predictive feedback can serve as an effective approach for self-supervised training.

title: UniBlendNet: Unified Global, Multi-Scale, and Region-Adaptive Modeling for Ambient Lighting Normalization
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 NTIRE Workshop on New Trends in Image Restoration and Enhancement. 8 pages, 4 figures
summary: Ambient Lighting Normalization (ALN) aims to restore images degraded by complex, spatially varying illumination conditions. Existing methods, such as IFBlend, leverage frequency-domain priors to model illumination variations, but still suffer from limited global context modeling and insufficient spatial adaptivity, leading to suboptimal restoration in challenging regions. In this paper, we propose UniBlendNet, a unified framework for ambient lighting normalization that jointly models global illumination, multi-scale structures, and region-adaptive refinement. Specifically, we enhance global illumination understanding by integrating a UniConvNet-based module to capture long-range dependencies. To better handle complex lighting variations, we introduce a Scale-Aware Aggregation Module (SAAM) that performs pyramid-based multi-scale feature aggregation with dynamic reweighting. Furthermore, we design a mask-guided residual refinement mechanism to enable region-adaptive correction, allowing the model to selectively enhance degraded regions while preserving well-exposed areas. This design effectively improves illumination consistency and structural fidelity under complex lighting conditions. Extensive experiments on the NTIRE Ambient Lighting Normalization benchmark demonstrate that UniBlendNet consistently outperforms the baseline IFBlend and achieves improved restoration quality, while producing visually more natural and stable restoration results.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes UniBlendNet, a unified framework for ambient lighting normalization that improves image restoration under complex illumination conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for ambient lighting normalization suffer from limited global context modeling and insufficient spatial adaptivity, leading to suboptimal restoration results.
AI.method: The proposed UniBlendNet framework jointly models global illumination, multi-scale structures, and region-adaptive refinement using a UniConvNet-based module, Scale-Aware Aggregation Module (SAAM), and mask-guided residual refinement mechanism.
AI.result: UniBlendNet consistently outperforms the baseline IFBlend and achieves improved restoration quality, producing visually more natural and stable results on the NTIRE Ambient Lighting Normalization benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed UniBlendNet framework effectively improves illumination consistency and structural fidelity under complex lighting conditions, leading to better image restoration results.

title: TLoRA+: A Low-Rank Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Method for Large Language Models
comment: 16 pages, 12 figures and 6 tables in total. Submitted to CoLM
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) aims to adapt pre-trained models to specific tasks using relatively small and domain-specific datasets. Among Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) stands out by matching the performance of full fine-tuning while avoiding additional inference latency. In this paper, we propose a novel PEFT method that incorporates the TLoRA+ optimizer into the weight matrices of pre-trained models. The proposed approach not only preserves the efficiency of low-rank adaptation but also further enhances performance without significantly increasing computational cost. We conduct experiments on the GLUE benchmark across diverse model architectures. Numerical experiments consistently demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our proposed method.

AI.tldr: A novel Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) method is proposed, incorporating the TLoRA+ optimizer into pre-trained models, enhancing performance while preserving efficiency.
AI.motivation: To improve the performance of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods, specifically Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), without increasing computational cost.
AI.method: The proposed method integrates the TLoRA+ optimizer into the weight matrices of pre-trained models, maintaining low-rank adaptation efficiency while enhancing performance.
AI.result: The proposed method consistently demonstrates effectiveness and robustness across diverse model architectures on the GLUE benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The novel PEFT method proposed preserves the efficiency of low-rank adaptation while further enhancing performance, making it a viable alternative to existing methods.

title: A Multimodal Clinically Informed Coarse-to-Fine Framework for Longitudinal CT Registration in Proton Therapy
summary: Proton therapy offers superior organ-at-risk sparing but is highly sensitive to anatomical changes, making accurate deformable image registration (DIR) across longitudinal CT scans essential. Conventional DIR methods are often too slow for emerging online adaptive workflows, while existing deep learning-based approaches are primarily designed for generic benchmarks and underutilize clinically relevant information beyond images. To address this gap, we propose a clinically scalable coarse-to-fine deformable registration framework that integrates multimodal information from the proton radiotherapy workflow to accommodate diverse clinical scenarios. The model employs dual CNN-based encoders for hierarchical feature extraction and a transformer-based decoder to progressively refine deformation fields. Beyond CT intensities, clinically critical priors, including target and organ-at-risk contours, dose distributions, and treatment planning text, are incorporated through anatomy- and risk-guided attention, text-conditioned feature modulation, and foreground-aware optimization, enabling anatomically focused and clinically informed deformation estimation. We evaluate the proposed framework on a large-scale proton therapy DIR dataset comprising 1,222 paired planning and repeat CT scans across multiple anatomical regions and disease types. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, enabling fast and robust clinically meaningful registration.

AI.tldr: A new deformable registration framework for proton therapy that integrates multimodal information and achieves fast and robust clinically meaningful registration.
AI.motivation: Proton therapy requires accurate deformable image registration (DIR) across longitudinal CT scans, but conventional DIR methods are too slow for online adaptive workflows, and existing deep learning-based approaches underutilize clinically relevant information.
AI.method: A clinically scalable coarse-to-fine deformable registration framework that integrates multimodal information from the proton radiotherapy workflow, using dual CNN-based encoders, a transformer-based decoder, and incorporating clinically critical priors through anatomy- and risk-guided attention, text-conditioned feature modulation, and foreground-aware optimization.
AI.result: The proposed framework demonstrates consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods on a large-scale proton therapy DIR dataset, enabling fast and robust clinically meaningful registration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of deformable image registration in proton therapy, enabling more effective treatment planning and delivery.

title: Empirical Evidence of Complexity-Induced Limits in Large Language Models on Finite Discrete State-Space Problems with Explicit Validity Constraints
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly described as possessing strong reasoning capabilities, supported by high performance on mathematical, logical, and planning benchmarks. However, most existing evaluations rely on aggregate accuracy over fixed datasets, obscuring how reasoning behavior evolves as task complexity increases. In this work, we introduce a controlled benchmarking framework to systematically evaluate the robustness of reasoning in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) under progressively increasing problem complexity. We construct a suite of nine classical reasoning tasks: Boolean Satisfiability, Cryptarithmetic, Graph Coloring, River Crossing, Tower of Hanoi, Water Jug, Checker Jumping, Sudoku, and Rubik's Cube, each parameterized to precisely control complexity while preserving underlying semantics. Using deterministic validators, we evaluate multiple open and proprietary LRMs across low, intermediate, and high complexity regimes, ensuring that only fully valid solutions are accepted. Our results reveal a consistent phase transition like behavior: models achieve high accuracy at low complexity but degrade sharply beyond task specific complexity thresholds. We formalize this phenomenon as reasoning collapse. Across tasks, we observe substantial accuracy declines, often exceeding 50%, accompanied by inconsistent reasoning traces, constraint violations, loss of state tracking, and confidently incorrect outputs. Increased reasoning length does not reliably improve correctness, and gains in one problem family do not generalize to others. These findings highlight the need for evaluation methodologies that move beyond static benchmarks and explicitly measure reasoning robustness under controlled complexity.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) under increasing problem complexity, revealing a 'reasoning collapse' where models' accuracy sharply degrades beyond certain complexity thresholds.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate the robustness of LLMs' reasoning under progressively increasing problem complexity, as existing evaluations rely on aggregate accuracy over fixed datasets, which may not accurately reflect models' reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: The authors introduce a controlled benchmarking framework, constructing nine classical reasoning tasks with parameterized complexity, and evaluate multiple LLMs using deterministic validators across low, intermediate, and high complexity regimes.
AI.result: The results show a consistent 'phase transition like behavior' where models achieve high accuracy at low complexity but degrade sharply beyond task-specific complexity thresholds, with substantial accuracy declines and inconsistent reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the need for evaluation methodologies that move beyond static benchmarks and explicitly measure reasoning robustness under controlled complexity, as LLMs' reasoning capabilities are not as robust as previously thought.

title: Why Multimodal In-Context Learning Lags Behind? Unveiling the Inner Mechanisms and Bottlenecks
summary: In-context learning (ICL) enables models to adapt to new tasks via inference-time demonstrations. Despite its success in large language models, the extension of ICL to multimodal settings remains poorly understood in terms of its internal mechanisms and how it differs from text-only ICL. In this work, we conduct a systematic analysis of ICL in multimodal large language models. Using identical task formulations across modalities, we show that multimodal ICL performs comparably to text-only ICL in zero-shot settings but degrades significantly under few-shot demonstrations. To understand this gap, we decompose multimodal ICL into task mapping construction and task mapping transfer, and analyze how models establish cross-modal task mappings, and transfer them to query samples across layers. Our analysis reveals that current models lack reasoning-level alignment between visual and textual representations, and fail to reliably transfer learned task mappings to queries. Guided by these findings, we further propose a simple inference-stage enhancement method that reinforces task mapping transfer. Our results provide new insights into the mechanisms and limitations of multimodal ICL and suggest directions for more effective multimodal adaptation. Our code is available \href{https://github.com/deeplearning-wisc/Multimocal-ICL-Analysis-Framework-MGI}{here}.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes multimodal in-context learning (ICL) in large language models, finding that it performs well in zero-shot settings but degrades with few-shot demonstrations due to a lack of reasoning-level alignment between visual and textual representations.
AI.motivation: The extension of ICL to multimodal settings is poorly understood, and the authors aim to systematically analyze ICL in multimodal large language models.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic analysis of multimodal ICL, decomposing it into task mapping construction and task mapping transfer, and propose a simple inference-stage enhancement method.
AI.result: Multimodal ICL performs comparably to text-only ICL in zero-shot settings but degrades significantly under few-shot demonstrations. The proposed enhancement method reinforces task mapping transfer.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides new insights into the mechanisms and limitations of multimodal ICL, suggesting directions for more effective multimodal adaptation.

title: From Prediction to Justification: Aligning Sentiment Reasoning with Human Rationale via Reinforcement Learning
summary: While Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) systems have achieved high accuracy in identifying sentiment polarities, they often operate as "black boxes," lacking the explicit reasoning capabilities characteristic of human affective cognition. Humans do not merely categorize sentiment; they construct causal explanations for their judgments. To bridge this gap, we propose ABSA-R1, a large language model framework designed to mimic this ``reason-before-predict" cognitive process. By leveraging reinforcement learning (RL), ABSA-R1 learns to articulate the why behind the what, generating natural language justifications that ground its sentiment predictions. We introduce a Cognition-Aligned Reward Model (formerly sentiment-aware reward model) that enforces consistency between the generated reasoning path and the final emotional label. Furthermore, inspired by metacognitive monitoring, we implement a performance-driven rejection sampling strategy that selectively targets hard cases where the model's internal reasoning is uncertain or inconsistent. Experimental results on four benchmarks demonstrate that equipping models with this explicit reasoning capability not only enhances interpretability but also yields superior performance in sentiment classification and triplet extraction compared to non-reasoning baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ABSA-R1, a framework that enables Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) models to generate natural language justifications for their sentiment predictions, improving interpretability and performance.
AI.motivation: Current ABSA systems lack explicit reasoning capabilities, unlike human affective cognition, which constructs causal explanations for judgments.
AI.method: The authors propose ABSA-R1, a large language model framework that leverages reinforcement learning and a Cognition-Aligned Reward Model to generate natural language justifications for sentiment predictions.
AI.result: Experimental results on four benchmarks show that ABSA-R1 enhances interpretability and yields superior performance in sentiment classification and triplet extraction compared to non-reasoning baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework bridges the gap between ABSA systems and human-like reasoning, providing a more transparent and effective approach to sentiment analysis.

title: CausalDisenSeg: A Causality-Guided Disentanglement Framework with Counterfactual Reasoning for Robust Brain Tumor Segmentation Under Missing Modalities
summary: In clinical practice, the robustness of deep learning models for multimodal brain tumor segmentation is severely compromised by incomplete MRI data. This vulnerability stems primarily from modality bias, where models exploit spurious correlations as shortcuts rather than learning true anatomical structures. Existing feature fusion methods fail to fundamentally eliminate this dependency. To address this, we propose CausalDisenSeg, a novel Structural Causal Model (SCM)-grounded framework that achieves robust segmentation via causality-guided disentanglement and counterfactual reasoning. We reframe the problem as isolating the anatomical Causal Factor from the stylistic Bias Factor. Our framework implements a three-stage causal intervention: (1) Explicit Causal Disentanglement: A Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) coupled with an HSIC constraint mathematically enforces statistical orthogonality between anatomical and style features. (2) Causal Representation Reinforcement: A Region Causality Module (RCM) explicitly grounds causal features in physical tumor regions. (3) Counterfactual Reasoning: A dual-adversarial strategy actively suppresses the residual Natural Direct Effect (NDE) of the bias, forcing its spatial attention to be mutually exclusive from the causal path. Extensive experiments on the BraTS 2020 dataset demonstrate that CausalDisenSeg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and consistency across severe missing-modality scenarios. Furthermore, cross-dataset evaluation on BraTS 2023 under the same protocol yields a state-of-the-art macro-average DSC of 84.49.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CausalDisenSeg, a novel framework for robust multimodal brain tumor segmentation from incomplete MRI data by using causality-guided disentanglement and counterfactual reasoning.
AI.motivation: The robustness of deep learning models for multimodal brain tumor segmentation is compromised by incomplete MRI data due to modality bias, where models exploit spurious correlations rather than learning true anatomical structures.
AI.method: The proposed framework, CausalDisenSeg, uses a three-stage causal intervention: (1) Explicit Causal Disentanglement using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) with an HSIC constraint, (2) Causal Representation Reinforcement using a Region Causality Module (RCM), and (3) Counterfactual Reasoning with a dual-adversarial strategy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the BraTS 2020 dataset show that CausalDisenSeg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and consistency across severe missing-modality scenarios, achieving a state-of-the-art macro-average DSC of 84.49 on BraTS 2023.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CausalDisenSeg framework achieves robust segmentation via causality-guided disentanglement and counterfactual reasoning, addressing the vulnerability of deep learning models to incomplete MRI data.

title: MERRIN: A Benchmark for Multimodal Evidence Retrieval and Reasoning in Noisy Web Environments
comment: First three authors contributed equally. Project Page: https://merrin-benchmark.github.io/
summary: Motivated by the underspecified, multi-hop nature of search queries and the multimodal, heterogeneous, and often conflicting nature of real-world web results, we introduce MERRIN (Multimodal Evidence Retrieval and Reasoning in Noisy Web Environments), a human-annotated benchmark for evaluating search-augmented agents. MERRIN measures AI agents' ability to identify relevant modalities, retrieve multimodal evidence, and perform multi-hop reasoning over noisy web sources. It differs from prior work in three important aspects: (1) using natural language queries without explicit modality cues, (2) incorporating underexplored modalities such as video and audio, and (3) requiring the retrieval of complex, often noisy or conflicting multimodal evidence during web search. We evaluate diverse search agents powered by ten models, including strong closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4-mini, Gemini 3/3.1 Flash/Pro) and open-weight models (Qwen3-4B/30B/235B), across three search settings (no search, native search, and agentic search). Our results show that MERRIN is highly challenging: the average accuracy across all agents is 22.3%, with the best-performing agent reaching only 40.1%. We further observe that while stronger agents like Gemini Deep Research achieve higher performance, gains are modest due to over-exploration; they take more steps and use more tools, but are often distracted by conflicting or partially relevant web content, leading to incorrect answers. Compared to humans, these agents consume more resources yet achieve lower accuracy, largely due to inefficient source selection and an overreliance on text modalities. These findings highlight the need for search agents capable of robust search and reasoning across diverse modalities in noisy web environments, making MERRIN a valuable testbed for evaluating such capabilities.

AI.tldr: Introduced MERRIN, a benchmark for evaluating search-augmented agents in noisy web environments, testing multimodal evidence retrieval and multi-hop reasoning.
AI.motivation: The complexity of search queries and the heterogeneous nature of web results necessitate a new benchmark for assessing AI agents' search and reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Evaluated diverse search agents powered by ten models across three search settings, using natural language queries and incorporating underexplored modalities like video and audio.
AI.result: MERRIN proved highly challenging with an average accuracy of 22.3% across all agents, and the best-performing agent achieving only 40.1%.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the need for search agents capable of robust search and reasoning across diverse modalities in noisy web environments.

title: DF3DV-1K: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Distractor-Free Novel View Synthesis
summary: Advances in radiance fields have enabled photorealistic novel view synthesis. In several domains, large-scale real-world datasets have been developed to support comprehensive benchmarking and to facilitate progress beyond scene-specific reconstruction. However, for distractor-free radiance fields, a large-scale dataset with clean and cluttered images per scene remains lacking, limiting the development. To address this gap, we introduce DF3DV-1K, a large-scale real-world dataset comprising 1,048 scenes, each providing clean and cluttered image sets for benchmarking. In total, the dataset contains 89,924 images captured using consumer cameras to mimic casual capture, spanning 128 distractor types and 161 scene themes across indoor and outdoor environments. A curated subset of 41 scenes, DF3DV-41, is systematically designed to evaluate the robustness of distractor-free radiance field methods under challenging scenarios. Using DF3DV-1K, we benchmark nine recent distractor-free radiance field methods and 3D Gaussian Splatting, identifying the most robust methods and the most challenging scenarios. Beyond benchmarking, we demonstrate an application of DF3DV-1K by fine-tuning a diffusion-based 2D enhancer to improve radiance field methods, achieving average improvements of 0.96 dB PSNR and 0.057 LPIPS on the held-out set (e.g., DF3DV-41) and the On-the-go dataset. We hope DF3DV-1K facilitates the development of distractor-free vision and promotes progress beyond scene-specific approaches.

AI.tldr: A large-scale dataset, DF3DV-1K, is introduced for benchmarking distractor-free radiance field methods, comprising 1,048 scenes with clean and cluttered images, and is used to evaluate and improve existing methods.
AI.motivation: The lack of a large-scale dataset with clean and cluttered images per scene for distractor-free radiance fields, which limits the development of this field.
AI.method: Introduction of DF3DV-1K dataset and benchmarking of nine recent distractor-free radiance field methods and 3D Gaussian Splatting; fine-tuning a diffusion-based 2D enhancer to improve radiance field methods.
AI.result: Identification of the most robust methods and the most challenging scenarios; average improvements of 0.96 dB PSNR and 0.057 LPIPS on the held-out set and the On-the-go dataset.
AI.conclusion: The DF3DV-1K dataset facilitates the development of distractor-free vision and promotes progress beyond scene-specific approaches.

title: CANVAS: Continuity-Aware Narratives via Visual Agentic Storyboarding
summary: Long-form visual storytelling requires maintaining continuity across shots, including consistent characters, stable environments, and smooth scene transitions. While existing generative models can produce strong individual frames, they fail to preserve such continuity, leading to appearance changes, inconsistent backgrounds, and abrupt scene shifts. We introduce CANVAS (Continuity-Aware Narratives via Visual Agentic Storyboarding), a multi-agent framework that explicitly plans visual continuity in multi-shot narratives. CANVAS enforces coherence through character continuity, persistent background anchors, and location-aware scene planning for smooth transitions within the same setting We evaluate CANVAS on two storyboard generation benchmarks ST-BENCH and ViStoryBench and introduce a new challenging benchmark HardContinuityBench for long-range narrative consistency. CANVAS consistently outperforms the best-performing baseline, improving background continuity by 21.6%, character consistency by 9.6% and props consistency by 7.6%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CANVAS, a multi-agent framework for maintaining visual continuity in long-form visual storytelling, outperforming baselines in character consistency, background continuity, and props consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing generative models fail to preserve continuity across shots in long-form visual storytelling, leading to inconsistent characters, environments, and abrupt scene transitions.
AI.method: CANVAS, a multi-agent framework that plans visual continuity through character continuity, persistent background anchors, and location-aware scene planning.
AI.result: CANVAS outperforms baselines by 21.6% in background continuity, 9.6% in character consistency, and 7.6% in props consistency on three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CANVAS effectively improves visual continuity in long-form visual storytelling, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks.

title: Physically-Guided Optical Inversion Enable Non-Contact Side-Channel Attack on Isolated Screens
summary: Noncontact exfiltration of electronic screen content poses a security challenge, with side-channel incursions as the principal vector.
